learning_objective: Compute the cardinal value of a set.

feedback.incorrect: Count each distinct element once. Cardinality is the number of elements in the set, not the number of characters or duplicates.

learning_objective: Differentiate between finite and infinite sets.
stem: Let S = {n^2 | n is a member of ℕ}. What is the cardinal value of set S?
options: undefined -- cardinality only applies to finite sets
distractor_rationale: correct: f(n) is injective on N, so the image is countably infinite
mistakes the formula's constant for the cardinality
confuses infinite with empty
wrongly claims cardinality doesn't apply to infinite sets -- the text explicitly defines aleph-null for exactly this case

feedback.correct: Correct: n ↦ n^2 is injective on ℕ, so S has the same cardinality as ℕ itself -- countably infinite, ℵ₀.
feedback.incorrect: Because every natural number n produces a distinct value of the formula, S has exactly as many elements as ℕ does -- countably infinite (ℵ₀), not a finite count and not empty.

notes: Correctness follows from injectivity of the chosen formula on N, which holds for all three formula kinds used here by construction.

stem: Which of the following correctly represents the set of all integer multiples of 10 that are greater than zero, using set-builder notation?
answer: {10n | n is a member of ℕ}
options: {10n | n is a member of ℕ}
{10n | n is an element of ℤ}
{n10 | n is a member of ℕ}
{10n + 1 | n is a member of ℕ}
distractor_rationale: correct: kn for n in N is exactly the positive multiples of k
swapped multiplication for exponent-style juxtaposition -- not valid notation
uses ℤ instead of ℕ, which wrongly allows zero and negative multiples
wrong formula -- shifts every value by 1, no longer 'multiples of k'

feedback.correct: Correct: {10n | n is a member of ℕ} generates exactly 10, 20, 30, ... -- the positive multiples of 10.
feedback.incorrect: 'Multiples of 10 greater than zero' means 10 times each natural number starting at 1 -- domain must be ℕ (not ℤ, which would include 0 and negatives).

notes: Correct answer and all distractors are computed from k; no factual content.

stem: Represent the set of all real numbers using set-builder notation, and explain in one or two sentences why the roster method (even with an ellipsis) is not a practical way to represent this set.
answer: {x | x is a real number}. Explanation: the real numbers are uncountably infinite and have no natural 'next' element to establish a listing pattern, so no roster/ellipsis listing can represent them.
notes: Essay, manually graded. Rubric: set-builder correct (1 pt), explanation references uncountability/no listing pattern (1 pt).

learning_objective: Represent sets in a variety of ways.
stem: Determine the best way to represent the set of all integers, and write it using that method. Include an ellipsis.
answer: {..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, ...}  (roster method with a leading AND trailing ellipsis, since the integers are unbounded in both directions)

feedback.correct: Correct -- the integers are infinite in both directions, so the roster method needs an ellipsis at both ends: {..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, ...}.
feedback.incorrect: The set of integers has no smallest or largest value, so unlike ℕ (which only needs a trailing ellipsis), ℤ needs an ellipsis at BOTH ends.

notes: Pairs with C-01 to give the #13-20 block two different infinite-set representation strategies (set-builder vs. double-ended-ellipsis roster), plus C-02 (empty) and C-03 (finite) -- covers all four flavors an assignment spec might ask for.

notes: Genuine real-world-fact judgment -- 'large' is not the same as 'infinite'; needs a human pass to confirm each category is truly bounded and that the rationale holds up.

learning_objective: Represent well-defined sets and the empty set with proper set notation.
stem: Represent the set of natural numbers divisible by zero, symbolically.

feedback.correct: Correct -- division by zero is undefined, so no natural number satisfies 'divisible by zero.' The set has no members: the empty set.
feedback.incorrect: No number can be divided by zero (it's undefined), so no natural number qualifies -- this set has zero elements, the empty set, written ∅ or { }.

notes: Second empty-set-trick instance, pairs with C-02 -- this one hinges on division-by-zero being undefined rather than a geometric impossibility.

learning_objective: Represent well-defined sets and the empty set with proper set notation.
stem: Represent the set of polar bears that live in Antarctica, symbolically.

feedback.correct: Correct -- polar bears are native to the Arctic (the opposite pole); none live in Antarctica in the wild, so this set has no members.
feedback.incorrect: Polar bears live in the Arctic, not Antarctica (that's penguins) -- no polar bear meets this description, so the set is empty: ∅ or { }.

notes: Third empty-set-trick instance -- this one hinges on a real-world biogeography fact rather than pure logic or an undefined operation, so it needs a fact-check, not just a logic-check.

learning_objective: Represent sets in a variety of ways.
stem: Determine the best way to represent the set of all songs written by Prince, and write the set using that method.
answer: {x | x is a song written by Prince}  (set-builder notation -- the set is well-defined and finite, but far too large to practically list by roster)

feedback.correct: Correct -- this is a well-defined, finite set (Prince wrote a specific, if very large, number of songs), but roster is impractical at that scale, so set-builder notation is the better choice.
feedback.incorrect: The set is well-defined and finite, but the roster method breaks down when a set is this large -- set-builder notation ({x | x is a song written by Prince}) represents it far more practically.

notes: New topic: method-choice for large-but-finite named collections -- the mirror case of method_choice.finite_word (small finite -> roster); here large finite -> set-builder.

stem: Determine the best way to represent the set of children's books written and illustrated by Mo Willems, and write the set using that method.
answer: {x | x is a children's book written and illustrated by Mo Willems}  (set-builder notation -- well-defined and finite, but impractical to roster in full)

feedback.incorrect: The set is well-defined and finite (a real author has a real, bounded catalog), but it's large enough that set-builder notation is more practical than a full roster.

notes: Second instance of the large-finite method-choice family, pairs with C-07.

learning_objective: Represent sets in a variety of ways.
stem: Determine the best way to represent the set of Mike and Carol's children on the TV show The Brady Bunch, and write the set using that method.
answer: {Greg, Marcia, Peter, Jan, Bobby, Cindy}  (roster method -- small, specific, finite set of named children)

feedback.correct: Correct -- six named children is small and finite, so the roster method is the practical choice: {Greg, Marcia, Peter, Jan, Bobby, Cindy}.
feedback.incorrect: List each of the six Brady Bunch children by name. Because the set is small and finite, roster method is the appropriate choice.

notes: Second instance of the small-finite-named-list method-choice family, pairs with C-03 (mississippi vowels). This is the item that used to be mislabeled as exercise 15 while actually testing a different word (mississippi) -- now the actual Brady Bunch content exists too.

learning_objective: Represent sets in a variety of ways.
stem: Determine the best way to represent the set of the seven colors commonly listed in a rainbow, and write the set using that method.
answer: {red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet}  (roster method -- small, specific, finite set of colors)

feedback.correct: Correct -- the traditional rainbow color list (ROYGBIV) is small and finite, so roster method is appropriate: {red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet}.
feedback.incorrect: List each of the seven traditionally named rainbow colors (ROYGBIV). Because the set is small and finite, roster method is the appropriate choice.

notes: Third instance of the small-finite-named-list method-choice family.
